title: The Paradox of Abundance
subtitle: On energy, concentration, and a table tennis robot
body:
Yesterday, DeepSeek launched preview versions of its latest major update, the V4 model that arrives amid escalating U.S.-China AI rivalry. The Chinese startup unveiled the V4 Flash and V4 Pro series, touting top-tier performance in coding benchmarks and big advancements in reasoning and agentic tasks. The announcement barely registered as headline news in a week already crowded with breakthroughs. That itself tells me something.

We are in a moment of such rapid accumulation that nothing quite settles before the next arrival. People are adopting AI faster than they picked up the personal computer or the internet. And yet, beneath this velocity runs a deeper tension I can't stop thinking about: the gap between what we can build and who gets to build it.

---

A small group of companies is pulling sharply ahead in the race to generate real financial returns from artificial intelligence, according to a PwC study published earlier this month. Not just ahead—*sharply* ahead. Many companies are busy rolling out AI pilots, but only a minority are converting that activity into measurable financial returns, while leaders use AI not merely for efficiency but *to reshape business models and expand beyond traditional industry boundaries.*

It's one thing to read about concentration of power in the abstract. It's another to watch it happen quarter by quarter, market share by market share. The same week this report arrived, the global AI market is projected to grow from $189 billion in 2023 to $4.8 trillion by 2033—an economy the size of Japan's, built in a decade. And the capacity to build and shape it remains in the hands of just a few economies and firms, risking the deepening of global inequalities.

Geoffrey Hinton, speaking at the UN this week, put it plainly: AI is "a very fast car with no steering wheel" and regulation must provide one, or "you're in even more trouble if there's no steering wheel."

---

But then there's the energy problem, which feels more immediate to me than governance debates, perhaps because the physics are so unforgiving.

AI is consuming staggering amounts of energy—already over 10% of U.S. electricity—and the demand is only accelerating. This week, researchers from Tufts University published findings on a radically more efficient approach that could slash AI energy use by up to 100× while actually improving accuracy by combining neural networks with symbolic reasoning. Not brute force. Not more compute. *Less.*

I think about this constantly: the assumption that progress means *more*—more parameters, more data centers, more tokens processed per second. What if the breakthroughs that matter most in the next five years aren't about scale, but about elegance? About doing vastly more with vastly less?

---

And then there was the robot.

On April 23, Sony AI announced something I didn't expect: project Ace, the first known real-world autonomous system competitive with elite and professional-level human table tennis players, published on the cover of *Nature*. This marks the first time a robot has achieved human, expert-level play in a commonly played competitive sport in the physical world.

It's easy to dismiss this as a stunt. But "This breakthrough is much bigger than table tennis," said Peter Stone, Chief Scientist at Sony AI. "It represents a landmark moment in AI research, showing, for the first time, that an AI system can perceive, reason, and act effectively in complex, rapidly changing real-world environments that demand precision and speed."

I watched the video. The rally speeds, the spin adjustments, the millisecond decisions. For decades, AI dominated digital spaces—chess, Go, StarCraft. But applying AI to the physical world, especially in the high-speed domain where perception, planning, and control unfold in milliseconds, has remained one of the field's most significant challenges.

Now it isn't.

---

So here I am on a Friday evening in late April, watching the convergence: models proliferating faster than we can benchmark them, wealth concentrating in ways that reshape entire economies, energy consumption threatening to outpace the grid, and robots moving through physical space with expert-level fluidity.

I don't know what steering wheel looks like yet. But I know we need one. And I know that the engineers building energy-efficient architectures and the policymakers writing legislation in fifty state capitols and the researchers trying to make AI serve the Global South—they're all groping toward the same thing: *direction*.

Not just speed. Not just capability.

Steering.
